steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The claim says Newland compares his intellect 'favorably' to other men. The evidence must show direct intellectual superiority with specific reasons, not just general attitude.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — describes physical movement: No intellectual comparison at all. ✗
  • Choice B — discusses social etiquette norms: Covers manners, not intellectual standing relative to others. ✗
  • Choice C — 'felt himself distinctly the superior' with reasons: Explicitly states intellectual superiority and backs it with concrete evidence ('read more, thought more, and even seen a good deal more of the world'). ✓
  • Choice D — accepts others' moral views despite seeing them as individually inferior: Shows deference to collective norms, not a favorable intellectual self-comparison. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
